book_summary: Joshua has chapters 1-8 complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. The opening movement covers Joshua's succession after Moses, Torah meditation and public courage, Rahab's intelligence and household sanctuary, the Jordan crossing, twelve-stone memorials, covenant circumcision and Passover, the commander's refusal of simplistic partisanship, Jericho's total destruction, Achan's theft and household execution, Ai's ambush and annihilation, and the Ebal Torah ceremony read to women, children, immigrants, leaders, and native-born residents. Maximum safeguards reject colonial conquest, genocide, child killing, siege against civilians, anti-sex-worker stigma, forced genital modification, leader worship, sacred looting, collective punishment, trial by lot, archaeological overclaiming, and nationalist memorial erasure.

chapters.notes: Succession after Moses, courage under Torah, day-and-night recitation, Jordan preparation, Transjordan obligation, and death-for-dissent language under anti-authoritarian safeguards.
Rahab the sex worker as strategic agent, concealment under royal threat, YHWH confession, covenant oath, scarlet cord, extended-household sanctuary, and anti-stigma safeguards.
Ark-led Jordan crossing, sacred distance, flood-stage water, priests standing in the dangerous middle, public confirmation of Joshua, and conquest framing under restraint.
Twelve-stone memorial, children's questions, priestly exit, armed east-Jordan contingent, Gilgal, Exodus comparison, and truthful-memory safeguards.
Kings' fear, wilderness-generation circumcision and recovery, Gilgal, Passover, cessation of manna, and the commander of YHWH's host answering Joshua's partisan question with No/Neither.

Jericho procession, wall collapse, herem killing of civilians, children, elders, and animals, Rahab's household rescue, sanctuary treasury metals, city burning, and rebuilding curse under moral protest.
Achan's hidden plunder, Ai defeat and thirty-six deaths, lot identification, confession, execution of household and animals, and categorical anti-collective-punishment safeguards.
Ai ambush, thirty-thousand/five-thousand textual tension, twelve thousand dead, city burning, king hanging and sunset burial, Ebal altar, Torah inscription, and inclusive public reading.

translation_notes: Joshua's authority is successor authority under Torah, not a blank check for charisma, conquest, or execution of dissent.
Land-boundary and sole-of-foot language is ancient promise rhetoric and may not function as a modern property deed or authorization for displacement.
Rahab's occupation must not be euphemized or stigmatized; her strategic intelligence, covenant negotiation, and household rescue are central.
The Jordan miracle is narratively joined to dispossession and cannot be used as proof that conquest is ethically validated.
Memorial stones should provoke children's questions and truthful memory rather than nationalist mythmaking that erases victims.
Circumcision is an ancient covenant rite; the chapter may not authorize coercive genital modification or shame uncircumcised bodies.
The commander saying No/Neither resists claims that Divine power automatically belongs to one's faction.
Jericho's herem explicitly kills men, women, children, elders, and animals and is categorically non-reenactable.
Rahab's rescue does not morally compensate for Jericho's dead.
Achan's theft requires accountability, but execution of his children, household, and animals is collective punishment and categorically rejected.
Ai's ambush, city burning, no-survivor language, plunder, and king execution remain ancient war violence under moral protest.
The Ebal reading gives Torah access to women, children, immigrants, leaders, and native-born residents; this constructive inclusion does not erase the massacre preceding it.
Mystical companion layers remain interpretive and never replace restored/source-audited text.
source_witness_audit_flags: Joshua 1 land boundaries, Torah-scroll terminology, and Transjordan troop obligations require Deuteronomy and Numbers comparison.
Joshua 2 zonah terminology, scarlet-cord symbolism, Rahab's household, and later Jewish/Christian reception require anti-stigma and source audit.
Joshua 3-4 Jordan geography, flood stage, Adam/Zarethan, duplicated twelve-stone traditions, and forty-thousand troop count require textual, archaeological, and hydrological audit.
Joshua 5 second-circumcision wording, Gibeath-haaraloth, Gilgal wordplay, manna cessation, and commander's No/Neither require philological and ritual-history audit.
Joshua 6 Jericho chronology, wall-collapse traditions, archaeological strata, herem terminology, treasury metals, and rebuilding curse require maximum historical and anti-genocide safeguards.
Joshua 7 corporate-guilt grammar, lot procedure, singular/plural execution wording, household culpability, and Valley of Achor reception require textual and legal-history audit.
Joshua 8 thirty-thousand versus five-thousand ambush figures, Bethel's role, twelve-thousand total, king-hanging procedure, and Ai archaeology require textual-witness and archaeological audit.
Joshua 8 Ebal/Gerizim altar and inscription traditions require comparison with Deuteronomy 27, Samaritan Pentateuch, Septuagint, and Masoretic witnesses.
next_actions: Continue Joshua in another large batch with chapters 9-16: Gibeonite deception and forced labor, southern coalition warfare and sun-still poetry, northern coalition and mass destruction, the defeated-kings list, aging Joshua and unconquered territories, east/west land distributions, Caleb's inheritance, Judah's borders and Achsah's water request, and Joseph-tribe allotments—with anti-slavery, anti-genocide, anti-colonial, gender-agency, astronomical-text, and archaeological safeguards.
